Checking for null is not the right way to check if something is supported or not:

> https://www.opengl.org/registry/specs/ARB/get_proc_address.txt
> The pointer returned should be cast to a function pointer type matching
    the extension function's definition in that extension specification.
    A return value of NULL indicates that the specified function does
    not exist for the implementation.
>   A non-NULL return value for glXGetProcAddressARB **does not guarantee
    that an extension function is actually supported at runtime**. The
    client must must also query glGetString(GL_EXTENSIONS) or
    glXQueryExtensionsString to determine if an extension is supported
    by a particular context.

Glfw also documents this behavior:
> http://www.glfw.org/docs/latest/group__context.html#ga35f1837e6f666781842483937612f163
> The address of a given function is not guaranteed to be the same between contexts.
This function may return a non-NULL address despite the associated version or extension not being available. Always check the context version or extension string first.

This means your check is flawed since the beginning you should rather check for the version/extension explicitly instead. 
To answer the question, it is not intended, but also not a bug (the technical reason: it just makes everything a lot easier).

@jdemeyer @vinklein 

I've been digging through a few inconsistencies with conversion to `mpfr` and `mpc` types. With the (intended) current design, `mpfr/mpc` instances that are created by `mpfr()` / `mpc()` will fit within the context's precision and exponent range. Temporary `mpfr/mpc` instances that are only used in a computation (should) use be converted exactly if possible. The precision/exponents may be different/larger than the context.

I am now convinced this design was a mistake. I would like to add a new context option that will select between two behaviors:

convert_exact = True

All `mpfr\mpc` conversions will convert exactly if possible (i.e. if the input is an integer or uses binary floating point). The decimal module uses this convention.

>>> from decimal import Decimal
>>> a=Decimal(1.2)
>>> a
Decimal('1.1999999999999999555910790149937383830547332763671875')
>>> +a
Decimal('1.199999999999999955591079015')
>>>

convert_exact = False

All conversions, even for temporary use, are restricted to the limits specified in the context. This is the behavior of gmpy2 2.0.

I would like to make `convert_exact=True` the default. I think the `ieee` contexts should use `convert_exact=False` since this more closely aligns with standard Python float behavior.

Hey @lingfish , yeah, the issue is more that Atlassian is moving to an entirely new system, tossing away any integrations developers have built - and the migration is forced.  HipChat is going to be killed.

It's also important to note that XMPP is the only real-time interface for HipChat - instead of an extra option as it is in Slack et al.  

In most big migrations like this for an enterprise provider, you'd provide a legacy backwards-compatible adapter so that your clients didn't have to spend resources rebuilding their business components because you want to improve your platform.  Or, you provide a clear, long deprecation and support schedule.

Why HipChat didn't choose to do either of those is beyond me, but it certainly cost them our business.  As mentioned, I'm very much a +1 to stride support if you or anyone else has interest!

Chris, in the just uploaded new master branch there is the requested improvement. Now your scenario is:

```shell
FoBiS.py build --track_build # build and track building, enable with also abbreviated switch -tb or by "track_build = True" into fobos
FoBiS.py install --prefix my_path # will install the previous built objects into my_path
tree my_path
└── bin
    └── hello
# or for library
tree my_path
├── include
│   └── library.mod
└── lib
    └── liblibrary.so
```

The sub-directories of prefix, `bin`, `lib` and `include` can be customized during the install command:

```shell
→ FoBiS.py install -h
usage: FoBiS.py install [-h] [-dbld BUILD_DIR] [-p PREFIX] [--bin BIN]
                        [--lib LIB] [--include INCLUDE] [-f FOBOS] [-fci]
                        [-mode MODE] [-lmodes] [--print_fobos_template]
                        [-force_compile] [-colors] [-l] [-graph] [-q]
                        [-verbose] [-j JOBS] [-m MAKEFILE_name]

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit

directories:
  -dbld BUILD_DIR, --build_dir BUILD_DIR
                        Directory containing built objects [default: ./]
  -p PREFIX, --prefix PREFIX
                        Prefix path where built objects are installed
  --bin BIN             Prefix sub-directory where executable files are
                        installed
  --lib LIB             Prefix sub-directory where library files are installed
  --include INCLUDE     Prefix sub-directory where include files are installed
...
```

I preferred to no modify the build command: build-structure can be a very personal taste, whereas install-structure is surely more *standardized*.

Hey xiuyanduan,
I have a bit of experience using pylatex and just saw this issue, maybe I can help you. 

> How to set the documentclass with some variables? 

- You can define the document class by using the pylatex Options
`from pylatex.base_classes.command import Options`
and set the document class like this:
`doc.documentclass.options=Options('12pt', 'svgnames', 'final')`


> How to add some text with \ or {} into the tex file?

- I think your problem is that firstly python escapes the backslashes and pylatex also in some occurences. You can avoid this by following the basic example provided in the pylatex examples using the pylatex NoEscape function with the prefix r before your string.

See here e.g.: [basic example](https://jeltef.github.io/PyLaTeX/latest/examples/basic.html)
here it's used like this:

> doc.append(NoEscape(r'\maketitle'))

In your case I would suggest you use
`doc.preamble.append(NoEscape(r'\definecolor{ColorName}{RGB}{76,73,72}'))`

Note that you have to import the NoEscape definition first:
`from pylatex import NoEscape`


> How to append \vspace and \hspace into a figure?

- Using the python `with` command you are basically inside the figure environment and using `doc.append(NoEscape(r''))` should do the trick (I guess)

```
with doc.create(Figure(position='htbp')) as plot:
    doc.append(NoEscape(r''))
    plt.plot(x,y)
    plot.add_plot(width=NoEscape(r'1\textwidth'))
```

Hope that helps to solve your problem. Let us know how this works out for you.

By the way, if you don't do this already, you can let pylatex generate your pdf document by e.g. calling
doc.generate_pdf(extra_compiler_args='--xelatex')

Regards,
Andreas

Original comment by: Jason R. Coombs

Of course this could be one way to solve the issue.

But I don't know if it's the best way to do it.

In many cases the keyring lib acts as a wrapper around specific keyring implementations - the user could do very little to recover from an error, since the exception would be highly backend-specific.

I think the common usage pattern would be "get this password if you can" and "save this password if you can", but very few users would expect an exception if something strange occurs, and would lead to *lot* of boilerplate code in the caller.

If you think a change in the current behaviour is unwanted we could write some utility functions (something like set_password_safely) that never raise exceptions, then letting the user to choose what it's best for him.

In this table, `play/pause media` has a scan code of the G key (34).

It is set that way, because `user32.MapVirtualKeyExW(179, MAPVK_VK_TO_VSC_EX, 0)` returns `57378` (179 is a vk code for play/pause). Then it is trimmed to 1 byte in here:

But `57378 & 0xFF` is `34` (scan code for G).

In the end I decided to use FS encoding everywhere and leave locale alone. I think it's more convenient because as long as psutil keep the promise to return fs-encoded strings everywhere the user has to think about and deal with 1 encoding only instead of 2.

Anyway, here's what changed with https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/pull/1052: 

* all strings are encoded by using the OS filesystem encoding which varies
  depending on the platform you're on (e.g. UTF-8 on Linux, mbcs on Win)
* unicode is now correctly supported on Windows (no invalid data) by using specific Windows unicode APIs
* no API call is supposed to crash with ``UnicodeDecodeError``
* instead, in case of badly encoded data returned by the OS, the following error handlers are used to replace the bad characters in the string:
    * Python 2: ``"replace"``
    * Python 3: ``"surrogatescape"`` on POSIX and ``"replace"`` on Windows
* on Python 2 all APIs return bytes (``str`` type), never ``unicode``
* on Python 2 you can go back to unicode by doing: 
  `unicode(p.exe(), sys.getdefaultencoding(), errors="replace")`

I think this is the best compromise in terms of usability for both Python 2 and 3: same types are returned, no information is lost.

I concur that it's inconvenient if your code is confused between byte strings (`bytes` in Python 3, `bytes` and its alias `str` in Python 2) and character strings (`str` in Python 3, `unicode` in Python 2).

But the alternative would be far worse: If this backport would accept bytes, that means if somebody fixes or amends your legacy code and uses the packed representation (`bytes` both on Python 2 and Python 3), then this backport would silently behave differently between Python 2 and Python 3 - if you're lucky, just throwing an exception, but in the worst case returning different results.

As much of the code using ipaddress is security-sensitive (firewalls, network management, etc.), unexpected behavior can have disastrous consequences.

Therefore, I must sadly close this report. You can either fix your code to use the correct data types on Python 2 and 3, or, if you are certain that a value is the representation of an ASCII-only byte string on Python 2 and a character string on Python 3, call [`six.u`](https://pythonhosted.org/six/#six.u) on it to get a character string all the time.

We've been reluctant to make changes without better tests in place so this issue has been lingering for a long while.

@kevinadi and I are wrangling a batch of changes for a 1.3.0 version at the moment and looking at how to approach this issue. A general thought we had in terms of testing sanity would be to create a script which can iterate versions of MongoDB and generate log snippets which can be used to compare mtools' logevent analysis for differences in interpretation. Does that sound like something you might have time to work on? If so, we should break out the test scenarios into a separate GitHub issue.

FYI: to reduce the surface area of what we need to support & test, we are only going to test non End-of-Life versions of MongoDB going forward (so 3.0+) as well as Python 2.7. The older logs have even more strange variations (eg. no years in the timestamps, blank lines, ... ).

Thanks,
Stennie

There are two things client/server can wait: HEADERS and DATA. I think we should put some indicator into the `grpclib.protocol.Stream.__headers__` queue in the `grpclib.protocol.Stream.__ended__` method to wake up `recv_headers` coroutine. There is a corresponding issue - #8.

I think it is necessary to distinguish normal `Stream.__ended__()` and other causes (errors). So when connection is lost, we should raise exceptions in `recv_headers` and `recv_data` coroutines.

1. `vim -o --remote-wait <file>` doesn't work as well. (Although it acts a bit different: it throws no error and ignores the `-o`).
1. It's documented that `-o` must take at least one argument.

I don't say it's impossible, but basically you want `-o` to take optional arguments and when no arguments are given act as a hint for `--remote-wait` to use `:split` instead of `:edit`.

So, I'd rather make it consistent with Vim and ignore the `-o` in that case.

But I guess that doesn't solve what you're trying to do, since `--remote-expr` is executed after `--remote-wait` on purpose. Should we introduce `--remote-split-wait` and friends?

@BoltonBailey for your information: I have released 0.0.15 which changes the behaviour of support enumeration to better allow for degenerate games.

This is now the behaviour with your example:

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import nashpy as nash
>>> A = np.matrix([[ 0, -1,  1,  1],
...                [ 1,  0, -1, -1],
...                [-1,  1,  0, -1],
...                [-1,  1,  1,  0]])
>>> game = nash.Game(A)
>>> list(game.support_enumeration())
[(array([  3.33333333e-01,   3.33333333e-01,   1.85037171e-17,
           3.33333333e-01]),
  array([  3.33333333e-01,   3.33333333e-01,   1.85037171e-17,
           3.33333333e-01]))]
```

There is some information about this here: https://nashpy.readthedocs.io/en/v0.0.15/reference/degenerate-games.html

Wow, that's some fancy LaTeX wizardry.  The `\label` marker is being used as literal text.  The difficulty for pandoc-eqnos is that the `\label` marker comes from an id attribute.  Spaces are not allowed in id attributes by either the html spec or pandoc.  As such, I really can't support them here.

The main problem I see is that LaTeX doesn't have good support for Lists of Equations (unlike for either figures or tables).  The reason for this, I imagine, is that equations normally don't have captions.

Perhaps some markdown like this would suffice:

    List of Equations
    -----------------

     1. Eq. {@eq:id1}: Write caption here.

     2. Eq. {@eq:id2}: Write caption here.

Although the list isn't automatically generated, there is the advantage that this will work with all of pandoc's output formats -- a key goal of pandoc-fignos.

> At the very least the convert function will eventually need to return a custom class (or two or three) that properly encapsulates the concept that DATE can return a datetime or a datetime and a timedelta...

I appreciate you looking into this, but I think I'm inclined to disagree.

If Alexa asks, 

"What day would you like to book your trip?" 

and the user says,

"This weekend", "This decade", or "Next year"

I think it's reasonable for skills to default to the beginning instant of those. 

Similarly, if users said they wanted, "This Tuesday", we wouldn't want to give 

02-28-2017:00:00:00 to 02-28-2017:23:59:59 as a conversion.

I'm not saying that it couldn't be done that way, but it's easier from an API client perspective if you just dish out dates and then have Alexa ask for refinements like AMAZON.Time or AMAZON.Duration as follow up questions.

It could be argued that a date represents a particular instant in time in computer parlance (i.e. database record, Python datetime, java.util.Date) and that decades and weekends are not dates; therefore, a decade needs to be converted into a date if the data type is AMAZON.Date. A date should not necessarily be converted into a duration.

That's my line of reasoning. Let me know what you think.

About the issue itself: i don't mind having to implement myself primitive builder for custom types. 
But I still feel sad that pyswagger do not support *swagger defined primitives* itself, because they are a bit edgy (`number/int32` and `number/int64` which are defined in the spec by the fact that number includes integer). 

Imho, you _should_ support these: because swagger-codegen didn't throw any errors with that type/format, neither does other libs like bravado for example... and swagger validators do not yell at these either.

Just one comment that does not apply to final result, and may not improve too much speed, but when you use `unravel_index`, you are finding the exact index (row,col) in correlation matrix, which it is the `hall_of_fame` variable where `fitness` values were sorted previously.
So as the correlation matrix rows/cols are ordered based on `fitness`, the maximum value of `unravel_index` is the worst that has to be used to delete. I mean, you can write it in the following way:
```
            [...]
            while len(components) > self.n_components:
                most_correlated = np.unravel_index(np.argmax(correlations),
                                                   correlations.shape)
                if self._metric.greater_is_better:
                    worst = max(most_correlated)
                else:
                    worst = min(most_correlated)
                components.pop(worst)
                indices.remove(worst)
                correlations = correlations[:, indices][indices, :]
                indices = list(range(len(components)))
            self._best_programs = [self._programs[-1][i] for i in
                                   hall_of_fame[components]]

        return self
```
But as I said, it does not improve the result as it makes the same operations.

@joshmarshall You might prefer to merge https://github.com/joshmarshall/jsonrpclib/pull/43 first.

It turns out that my original PR wasn't passing tests. My bad. I did a little more work trying to get it over the line but then it occurred to me that I might be flogging a dead horse. Here's why:

`jsonrpclib` has been specifically designed to mirror `xmlrpclib` and this works really well in py2.x. But in py3k `xmlrpclib` has been split into `xmlrpc.client` and `xmlrpc.server`, so the paradigm is broken.

What's really required is an entirely new, py3k-only package called `jsonrpc` containing the modules `jsonrpc.client` and `jsonrpc.server`. Unfortunately this name is already taken by an [old package](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/jsonrpc/1.2) which hasn't been updated since 2012 and doesn't seem to by py3k-compatible.

The most up-to-date JSON-RPC package is currently [json-rpc](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/json-rpc/) and it does support py3k, though it does not attempt to mirror the py3k `xmlrpc` package.

My original goal was a JSON-RPC implementation of Tornado's `WebsocketHandler` so I think I might go down the path of writing one based on `json-rpc`.

Here is the proposed fix, implemented in edb6b0020b26f431bee3ba085d1bea8a232a04f6 (and released as 1.6.6):
```python
handle.getASCIIStringDescriptor(handle.getDevice().getSerialNumberDescriptor())
```
The idea of `USBDevice` is that it gives access to what can be accessed without opening the device: what the OS has cached and exposes after it enumerated the device. Opening an `USBDevice` means producing an `USBHandle`, so to me the fix is rather to call methods available on `USBHandle`.

`getSerialNumber` along with `getManufacturer` and `getProduct` are exceptions to this rule, and rather intended as shortcuts to be used while looking for a device.

Sadly, I did not expose the raw descriptor ids for these 3, which is what I just added in 1.6.6 .

First, since TFoS is mostly just setting up the TF cluster, the ability to load a pre-trained model is no different than TensorFlow's ability to load a pre-trained model.

However, with that said, there are differences between single-node models and distributed models, so you would inference using parallel single-node instances or a distributed TFoS cluster, respectively.

For single-node models, you can look at [this code](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/examples/mnist/spark/mnist_spark_pipeline.py#L90) as an example.

For distributed models, you will most likely need the original training code to recreate/load the cluster/network for inferencing, esp. if device placement is significant.

@zheng-da Well, you can vary the cores up or down just to change the timing and try to make the race condition happen more often by changing the timing to a point where it's especially aggravated.

I have found, however, that reducing the number of cores that actually run the program (not the number of threads) can cause more overlap between threads.  By this, I mean, each thread gets a smaller timeslice of actual execution, and so things that might "overlap" tend to overlap with more frequency.  Think of two trains passing each other in opposite directions (threads).  If the trains are going fast, they are only next to each other for a short period of time.  But if the two trains are going slowly, then they are next to each other for a much longer period of time.

Hi @emmett9001, I explain briefly my use case, so to give you a bigger picture of my issue with the current implementation.

Kafka is currently part of our ad serving pipeline (at viralize.com), we are currently developing a new analytics solution that works more or less like this:

```
adservers -> log (kafka) -> sessionizer -> datapoints (kafka)
```

sessionizer is a consumer whose job is to enrich the data from the raw event logs, discard invalid events, run some fraud detection algorithms and produce datapoints for a timeseries database. The balanced consumer looks like a perfect fit for us in order to distribute the sessionizer processing.

sessionizer main loop is kind of:

```python
while True:
    batch = consume_batch()
    datapoints = process(batch)
    commit()
    produce(datapoints)
```

`process` makes use of a status storage. The storage is partitioned the same way as the kafka source, such that each node owns the same log and status partition. This way the nodes can cache the status locally for better performance.

I have the necessity to get an at most once delivery and absolutely avoid duplicates. What I'm gonna do now is:

- extend the BalancedConsumer class to add a pre-rebalance hook
- extend the topic class to return my new BalancedConsumer class
- extend the client class to return my new Topic class
- and change main loop slightly:

```python

rebalance_lock = threading.Lock()
def on_rebalance_start():
    rebalance_lock.acquire()
    flush_status()  # flushes the cache enabling other nodes to get the ownership

consumer.on_rebalance_start(on_rebalance_start)
consumer.on_rebalance_end(lambda: rebalance_lock.release())

while True:
    with rebalance_lock:
         batch = consume_batch()
         ...
```

The lock will let the consumer finish its current batch job before releasing the partition ownership.

I understand this is probably a specific use case still there are some flaws in the current design of pykafka in my opinion:

- subclassing a consumer is not easily supported by the current api (as there is not a `get_custom_consumer` method and I think subclassing all the other involved classes is a complexity that could be avoided)
- when the caller wants to manage the commit manually (by setting auto_commit_enabled to False) probably does not expect an automatic commit in case of rebalance.
- rebalancing cannot be synchronized with the other process threads (the reason why I'm introducing the pre rebalance hook in my subclass)

Probably it should be more explicit in the documentation how to get an at-least-once or at-most-once delivery. The current design doesn't permit any of the two and this wasn't that clear to me without looking at the code. (It is actually also a bit subtle, that's why I've opened this issue, I wasn't sure it was the expected behavior).

Prefixes are more accessible than namespaces. By accessible I mean easily discovered by a naive user. Our design across the package is basically to have a single level of bins within which are many similar functions. People using this package don't expect to have to traverse an additional level to find what they need. Ergo, functions sharing a prefix (which is also consistent with other, past design decisions) are more consistent with the typical user experience. There are points where this would break down, of course. If we had 500 functions in `measure` things would be different.

I'm not sure we'll have to deprecate this. Structural similarity is still the best of the bunch, and we don't need to throw the kitchen sink at this problem. Right now there are four functions which basically do the same thing, and I don't see a pressing need to add more.

At least, that's what is going through my head.

Looks like specifying the custom delimiter is not support from the command line. But we default to replacing `\001` with `\t` when `getresult` is called from the command line: https://github.com/qubole/qds-sdk-py/blob/v1.9.0/bin/qds.py#L109

So, you can just call
```
python qds.py hivecmd getresult 16010614
```
and you would get tab separated columns.

---

About your previous comment,

1. I agree the switching is unexpected. But you shouldn't notice it if you are using the command line because as I said above we automatically replace `001` with `\t`. And if you call the python methods directly, you can avoid it by specifying the custom `delim` as `\t`.

2. This is a known issue. I have rekindled the discussion to give an option to users to have the header `\001` delimited.

Thanks @dbs and @joernhees. I have made the change in the https://github.com/RDFLib/pyrdfa3 repo (see https://github.com/RDFLib/pyrdfa3/pull/13). The https://github.com/RDFLib/PyRDFa is explicitly marked as 'outdated', so I did not touch it.

RDF Interfaces is not a standard. It is a WG Note, which explicitly states, in the beginning of the document, that it is incomplete.

> This document is not finished.

And:

> A number of design issues have not been completely resolved. Developers wishing to implement this API should be aware of incomplete nature of the specification.

For instance, there are names in the spec which do not correspond to names for concepts in RDF 1.1 (like "nominalValue"), and ideas which have been further developed into specs (e.g. the notion of Profiles in this Note, to be compared with standardized JSON-LD Contexts).

(I have cautioned implementers of that Note of this fact before.) *If* the current RDFJS cooperation effort yields a stable and usable API, it might be valuable to have an implementation in Python. RDFLib might provide an implementation for that when that happens.

(Note that I think what RDF Interfaces attempted to to was very valuable, only that it is not complete. I fear that claiming that it is might cause confusion and impede effective interoperability (if interfaces do not conveniently support existing practices).)

Just in case anyone is thinking about it already, don't review this yet.  @cwwalter suggested another feature that would make sky images even faster, so I'm going to add that to this branch.

Specifically, we could have the Silicon class fill an image with the net area of each pixel.  Then, rather than rain down photons and decide which pixel each falls in, we can just take the areas of each pixel and use that times the surface brightness as the mean of a Poisson realization.

I think this idea will even work for high surface-brightness flats.  We can add this sky flux back into the Silicon object, get new areas that include B/F correlations, and repeat until we have the full sky level we want for the flat.  Should be loads faster than raining photons.

- Any loaded variable MUST be computable, for any period.
- Values of any variable MUST be specifiable, for any period.

- Headers MUST provide information on the loaded country package and its version.
- Headers COULD provide information on the core version.

- Any error in the input format, be it syntactic or semantic, MUST yield a 400.
- Errors in the computation MUST yield a 500 and expose the error to the user with as much context as possible.
- Misnamed variables and parameters SHOULD yield a 404, in order to ease legislation upgrades. 

### Documentation

- The API MUST be documented in the OpenAPI description.

I believe this is exactly what PR #264 implements, a function to calculate latitude and longitudes from a grid object either using an internal implementation of the azimuthal equidistant projection or a Proj projection, can you confirm this @gamaanderson? for anyone stumbling on this and looking for an interface from Python MR to Java Shuffler... I copied over the code for the delivered `MapreducePipeline` and substituted in this custom shuffler pipeline:

```
class JavaShufflePipeline(Pipeline):
  async = True

  def run(self, job_name, mapper_params, filenames, shards=None):
    # TODO: task payload will blow up if the filenames list is too long (can only be like 100k)
    bucket_name = mapper_params["bucket_name"]
    queue_name = os.environ.get("HTTP_X_APPENGINE_QUEUENAME")

    # build shuffleServlet call
    shuffleParams = {
      'shufflerQueue': queue_name,
      'gcsBucket': bucket_name,
      'inputFileNames': [f[len(bucket_name)+2:] for f in filenames],
      'outputDir': '{name}/{id}/shuffle'.format(name=job_name, id=self.root_pipeline_id),
      'outputShards': shards or len(filenames),
      'callbackQueue': queue_name,
      'callbackModule': modules.get_current_module_name(),
      'callbackVersion': modules.get_current_version_name(),
      'callbackPath': self.get_callback_url()
    }

    log.info('/shufflerServlet - {}'.format(shuffleParams))
    taskqueue.add(url='/shufflerServlet', payload=json.dumps(shuffleParams), target="wherever_you_deployed_the_shuffler", queue_name="your_queue_name")

  def callback(self, job, status, output=None):
    log.info("JavaShufflePipeline.callback(job={}, status={}, output={})".format(job, status, output))
    if status == 'failed':
      self.abort()
      self.complete()
    else:  # status == 'done'
      filename = '/' + app_identity.get_default_gcs_bucket_name() + '/' + output
      with gcs.cloudstorage.open(filename) as fp:
        filenames = fp.read()
      filenames = [fn for fn in filenames.split('\n') if fn]
      self.complete(filenames)
```

That said, creating a new object on top of an old object overwrites the old object, so you can do an extremely limited mimic of append by writing a separate object and composing the original and the separate object with a destination of the original object. Hi @tseaver,

Answers to questions:

> Do we need to support de novo creation of BucketNotfication instances w/ a user-supplied ID? The spec says that the ID is generated during notifications/insert, which suggests not.

Indeed, GCS will generate IDs for you.

> I assume that access to the new features does not depend on being able to import google.cloud.pubsub. The counter-example would be that BucketNotification.create must verify the existence of its topic, and maybe create a new one. Or does the back-end do that? The docs say that a topic is created if it does not exist.

GCS will not create a Cloud Pub/Sub topic for you if one does not exist. It will, though, check to see if the topic you specify exists and permissions have been appropriately configured for GCS to be able to publish topics to it, and the call to create a subscription will fail if either of these two requirements are not met. The "gsutil" command-line utility will, on the client side, create a topic if it does not exist.

I suggest a similar, single "subscribe()" command exist, that fetches the correct robot name, attaches appropriate permissions for it to a Pub/Sub topic (and maybe creates that topic), and then creates the subscription. Otherwise pretty much every user will have to write out that same chain of commands.

> There is no documented support for update / patch of existing notifications. Is that intentional?

Yes. Subscriptions cannot be modified. They can only be deleted and created.

> What are the intended semantics of passing {'payload_format`: 'NONE'}? I don't think subscribers will find message with an empty payload useful, so I must be misunderstanding something. Do the non-payload attributes provide enough information for the subscriber to do something useful?

The attributes provide enough information to accomplish most tasks. They tell you the event that happened (an object was created, an object was deleted), and they also tell you which object it happened to and when. The payload may be useful if you want to see, for instance, the object's type or its size, but it's not necessary in many cases.

> Another question: do we need a "round trip" system test? E.g., one which verifies that notifications arrive at the subscriber? Or can we get away with just doing BucketNotification CRUD in the system tests?

A round trip test would be ideal, but getting back a successful "subscription created" message from GCS is a good sign that you've done things correctly.

The tough ones are `as_future` and `call_later` as they don't have a nice way to just add a new `loop=None` kwarg.

We discussed a few options on IRC, and concluded:

 - breaking the API totally by adding a positional "loop" arg is Very Bad
 - `pop()` in a "loop" from kwargs is Bad (and will lead to weird, surprising errors in code that happens to already take a `loop` kwarg)
 - using something like the `testutils.replace_loop` context manager is weird and might not work reliably anyway

So we will try by introducing an "instantiate an API object"-type method (final naming TBD) that has all the `txaio` API methods hanging off it and takes an (optional) custom loop argument. So code like this:

```python
f0 = txaio.as_future(...)
f1 = txaio.call_later(...)
```

becomes:

```python
api = txaio.create_api(loop=asyncio.new_event_loop())
f0 = api.as_future(...)
f1 = api.call_later(...)
```

This won't change any existing code, but code that wants to use many custom loops can opt-in by using the `.create_api()` call.

@roll @akariv @callmealien @pwalsh to clarify: for the DM4T pilot, one of the datasets (ENLITEN) was provided as a [MySQL dump](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/pilot-dm4t/issues/1).  Given that one of the goals of this DM4T project more generally is to "make your data public to the rest of the public and beyond" (and that publishing a SQL dump is not super friendly), I thought there might be value in going straight from a SQL database directly to a Data Package.

I initially tried to use `jsontableschema-sql-py` [directly](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/pilot-dm4t/blob/master/enliten/sql_to_datapackage.ipynb), but:

Ok so I see the problem now.

Calling deisctl stop, call [`b.Stop`](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/deisctl/backend/fleet/stop.go#L12) which in turn calls [`b.doStop`](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/deisctl/backend/fleet/stop.go#L27). If an error happened in their, it wants to send an error to the errorChan.

On the other end, the stop call also starts a goroutine for [`printState`](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/deisctl/cmd/cmd.go#L461) which can return if it receives an empty output. The goroutine should only return if one (or both) of the channels are closed.

## Handling builtins

Related to https://github.com/blue-yonder/tsfresh/issues/8#issuecomment-257834295

Getting the codebase and tests aligned to run on both Python 2.7.x and
Python 3.5.x is going to require quite a bit of bifurcation so that imports are
handled correctly.  For example:

```
try:
  from builtins import zip
except ImportError:
  from future_builtins import zip
```

However this would have ramifications on the zip function possibly as per
https://docs.python.org/2/library/future_builtins.html

The use of builtins as it stands now in i8_add_python3_support is as follows:

tsfresh/tsfresh/feature_extraction/extraction.py - 1 builtins imports found
from builtins import str
tsfresh/tsfresh/feature_extraction/feature_calculators.py - 1 builtins imports found
from builtins import range
tsfresh/tsfresh/feature_extraction/settings.py - 4 builtins imports found
from builtins import zip
from builtins import str
from builtins import range
from builtins import object
tsfresh/tsfresh/examples/robot_execution_failures.py - 1 builtins imports found
from builtins import map
tsfresh/tsfresh/feature_selection/significance_tests.py - 1 builtins imports found
from builtins import str
tsfresh/tsfresh/feature_selection/feature_selector.py - 3 builtins imports found
from builtins import zip
from builtins import range
tsfresh/tsfresh/feature_selection/settings.py - 1 builtins imports found
from builtins import object
tsfresh/tests/transformers/test_feature_selector.py - 1 builtins imports found
from builtins import range
tsfresh/tests/feature_extraction/test_feature_calculations.py - 1 builtins imports found
from builtins import range

However at the moment would possible to just handle builtins with a try and
an innoccous except, maybe something like e.g.

```
try:
    from builtins import zip
except ImportError:
    from sys import version_info
```

Perhaps there is scope to add

```
from __future__ import absolute_import
```

As per http://python-future.org/compatible_idioms.html#imports-relative-to-a-package
this would make Py2 code safer (more like Py3) by preventing implicit relative
imports.

I am not certain of your thoughts on this.  Porting to Python 3 is a lot of
"little" things, but little things can have bigger implications on design, style
and function.

I am certain this port to Python 3 is going to raise lots of little questions,
which have a number of design and possibly even performance/refactor issues that
may need to be addressed.

I solved this by first changing directories to the folder where the yml file locates.

- Open terminal, type `cd` followed by a space.
- Then open finder, drag and drop the folder to the terminal window. (Drop the folder where the file - locates, not the file itself.)
- You should be able to see terminal showing code like this `cd /Users/username/path/to/my/projects/folder/`, press enter.
- You'll now locates at the folder where your yml file locates.
- Type the conda command again `conda env create -f environment.yml` (Replace enviornment.yml with the name of your yml file.)
- Viola!

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

For more detail, see [this blog post](http://takluyver.github.io/posts/i-cant-import-it.html).

The problem here is the Keras ResNet model that you use. It expects BGR images. Foolbox doesn't care whether it's RGB, but everything we put in and get out will be in the same format.

By using `image[:, :, ::-1]` we give Foolbox a BGR image that it passes along to the ResNet model. Note that Foolbox has no clue whether you passed it an RGB or BGR image. The adversarial returned by the attack is then in the same format as the one passed in, so `adversarial` and `image[:, :, ::-1]` have the same format, namely BGR in this case.

For the public API I'd envisage the following new functions: 
* `read_plink()` analogous to [read_vcf()](https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/master/allel/io/vcf_read.py#L172)
* `plink_to_npz()` analogous to [vcf_to_npz()](https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/master/allel/io/vcf_read.py#L283)
* `plink_to_hdf5()` analogous to [vcf_to_hdf5()](https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/master/allel/io/vcf_read.py#L477)
* `plink_to_zarr()` analogous to [vcf_to_zarr()](https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/master/allel/io/vcf_read.py#L695)

All of these functions could make use of a single internal API function `iter_plink_chunks()` analogous to [iter_vcf_chunks()](https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/master/allel/io/vcf_read.py#L830). This reads through the input files in fixed-length chunks (where the length of a chunk is expressed in terms of a number of variants) and loads data for each chunk into numpy arrays.

For VCF parsing there are loads of details related to which fields you want to extract, what data types you want to use, how many values to expect, etc., which just don't apply to plink files because you have a fixed set of fields and can assume biallelic variants and can fix datatypes. So hopefully implementation should be much simpler. 

The actual work of parsing a VCF file is done within the [allel.opt.io_vcf_read](https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/master/allel/opt/io_vcf_read.pyx) cython extension. Because VCF is text-based and a highly flexible format, I'm not sure there is much there we'd want to copy. The only performance-critical part for plink I expect will be reading and unpacking the plink binary genotypes into a numpy array, and so it may be that only a very small cython or C extension would be necessary and everything else can be done in Python.

As general examples of cython code, scikit-allel has two other cython extensions, [allel.opt.model](https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/master/allel/opt/model.pyx) has various data model computations (e.g., allele counting), and [allel.opt.stats](https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/master/allel/opt/stats.pyx) has some statistical functions.

Sure. You are correct, they are not independent. The main idea is to post these in stages, not all at once.

You start off by posting the cryptography engine pull request (1) and the KMIPProxy client pull request (2) first (since they don't directly relate to each other), including in each one everything needed to make the changes and tests work for those pull requests (for example, MACRequest/ResponsePayload would go in (2)). Once those two pull requests merge, you post the ProxyKmipClient pull request (3). Since (3) relies on (2), all of (3)'s dependencies will already be merged; all you need to do is make sure any remaining changes needed for (3) are included with it. Once (1), (2), and (3) are merged in, you can post the server pull request (4).

The main thing to watch out for here is including duplicate code across pull requests. Sometimes Git will handle this just fine but usually it will trigger a merge conflict, requiring you to go into the newer pull request and remove the duplicate code or restructure it so that it doesn't conflict with the rest of the code base.

This is generally how I try to add in new features that cross the clients and server, though I sometimes will do (2) and (3) in one patch if the additions are small enough. It varies. There are plenty of examples in the project commit history.

The core idea is to make things as easy for the developer and the reviewer(s) as possible, to help them ensure that the code is correct before officially merging it in. The larger a patch is, the easier it is for bugs to slip through. That's really all there is to it.

A local variable is already effectively "private." There is no need to precede it with an underscore to make it so. (Using a single leading underscore to convey privacy is only useful for otherwise visible attributes of other objects to convey, "Hey, this is my private attribute. Keep your mitts off.")

Ultimately, I think it boils down to the lack of configurability of pyflakes. There are plausible reasons to support both interpretations (sometimes both at different times or at different locations for the same piece of code), but it can only be one way. With pylint, despite the many false positives as it generates, its rich check suppression mechanism lets me have it both ways.

This clearly isn't my code base, so feel free to do what you want. You've had an open ticket for four years which took me a few minutes to fix the way I would have. If all you want to do is support a lone underscore, tweak the clause I added to restrict it to that case and be done with it.
What if `sum` has been redefined to mutate its parameter? In that case `sum( [ o for o in objs ] )` and `sum(objs)` would be very different things since the former effectively makes a copy.

Are you sure pyflakes is being run in Python 3.7? This is generally indicative of using the wrong version of Python, as pyflakes can only check for the Python version it is being run in. So if you use pyflakes in Python 3.5 or Python 2.7, then it won't know about f-strings and will report them as a syntax error.

I don't think this is appropriate in pep8-naming. This is something that *might* be better suited to Flake8, but would need a nuanced approach.

My main concern with adding this to pep8-naming is that other Flake8 plugins will adopt the same thing. I'm fairly certain optparse will complain if we try to doubly register the same option name with different descriptions so if this is something plugins want to ignore, it might be best to have it as a flag in Flake8 proper to have one flag to rule them all that plugins can use. That said, Flake8 generally does not provide flags by default that are no-op flags, so I'm not sure it makes sense there either.

> One other design question I have for this issue is whether or not there's utility to making the supported_versions be a class vs. instance variable of the concrete_subclasses.(...)



There is a major drawback to this, it leads to manually defining and changing parts of the code with each added TMOS version. For example:

- We define "supported_versions = ['11.6.0', '12.0.0'] " class attribute inside PathElement (this is required as some classes do not inherit from ResourceBase, Licensing class in /shared for example)

- Then in the classes that do not support version 11.6.0, we need to define their own class variable:
"supported_versions = ['12.0.0']". 

- Later on we add more versions to "supported_versions" class attribute inside PathElement, now, we have to go to each class that had their own "supported_versions" defined and edit the list, this has to be done each time we add new version.

With the current solution we just define "self._meta_data['supported_versions'] = ['11.6.0', '12.0.0']" in the PathElement, next we just do the following in classes that do not support 11.6.0:

"self._meta_data['supported_versions'].remove('11.6.0')"

Afterwards adding new classes to supported_versions will not require us to go back and change the above in each class that has been defined.

This is much easier to control as it grows and more scallable. Unless you know of another way we can do this then I am all ears :)

> I think there is a basic question here about what a "child product" is and how Oscar treats it. Until now child products were treated as "sub-products" - without their own detail page and projects were expected to handle them from the parent product page. I would ask what the rationale is for changing that, and if we do then it needs to be a carefully documented change.

As a result, it was not properly indicated price (or generally any of the variant-specific fields) when variant was selected on the parent product page.

But in fact, even though it could be treated as a "sub-product", variant can have its own set of attributes, its stock is tracked separately and now we will allow to manage images separately.
One way to handle this - update data (price, images) on the page within the variant selection, but I don't see the point to implement it in such way on existing Oscar codebase, since it's easily can be handled now via API and reactive frontend and hence I prefer solution, proposed by @mvantellingen - https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/pull/1643#issuecomment-97575119.

Although there's still place for backwards compatibility - we can just switch this property to False and allow developers to decide, whether they want to handle it via API or separate page.

I'm not sure if I understand you correctly. You do want to use the `b2_upload_part` API for large files (which I also would prefer)? If we do that and encrypt every part, as if it was a separate file using one CBC block and one IV, then we need to know the size of the individual parts to decrypt it later. The size could be stored as metadata but I would prefer if the size is also stored inside the file. 

The other alternative would be to split the file into lots of small chunks (like the proposed 4KB blocks) and encrypt them separately using CBC with different IVs. This would allow some sort of random access while encrypting/decrypting files, as each chunk can be processed individually. I would prefer this method.

For reference on this issue, it seems Windows [IOCP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Input/output_completion_port) (Input/Ouput Completion Ports) can't be used with the asyncio [ProactorEventLoop](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-eventloops.html#asyncio.ProactorEventLoop) without relying on private implementation details of the event loop. This [Stackoverflow issue](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/34866101/why-proactoreventloop-on-windows-not-working-with-serial-port) leads to more details and an alternative asyncio serial implementation which uses the aforementioned private interfaces.

> Will it be dominated by one of the loss objectives?

This depends on the objectives, but it's common to scale one of the losses (which adds a hyperparameter you need to tune). It can help to monitor both losses separately during training (e.g., have your training function return not only the total loss, but also the two components) so you can see what scales they are and how they develop. This gives you an idea on whether you should boost or weaken the influence of one of the losses over the other.

This is not really an ncclient issue, but more for the underlying SSH transport. As such, once you have successfully connected to the device and obtained a Manager object, `m`, you could try something like:

```
m._session._transport.set_keepalive(10.0)
```

This sets a keepalive time of 10 seconds on he underlying Paramiko transport used by ncclient. Be aware that the `_`-prepended variables are considered private and thus _may_ change in the future.

@sigmavirus24 I guess a similar option would be to have some sort of configuration you pass when setting up the `h11.Connection` object to choose the degree of header validation. (This would also be easier to implement+faster, since currently we do the header value validation as a side-effect of the regex's we use to pick apart the headers in the first place.) But if the person who has the problem is using, like, `someapp` → `requests` → `urllib3` → `h11`, then this kind of config doesn't help unless either `urllib3` sets it to the permissive mode by default, or else everyone in the stack provides their own config option that they then pass through to the next layer down. That's the motivation for thinking about envvars -- they're gross, but they do provide a way for a user to circumvent that stack.

That said... I guess we always want strict validation on outgoing headers, and that probably we always want strict validation as servers parsing incoming requests (because on the server side, you can give a proper error message, plus not-always-but-usually the client will be stuck working around whatever you do rather than vice-versa). And if this is breaking in real life and no extant clients actually enforce it, then I guess urllib3 and similar will probably want to disable it always anyway. So one heuristic would be: when parsing the headers from an incoming response, and *only* in this situation, then outlaw `\0`, `\r` and `\n` but let everything else through; otherwise (outgoing headers and incoming requests) apply full RFC validation.

Normally (or rather often), you use only one config-section in a configuration-file.
It contains the default-values for the command-line parameters or config-file only parameters.
The primary-section does not need a storage; it uses the default dictionary.
NOTE: The "primary section" is per default the first section that is specified in the ``config_sections`` attribute.

To avoid conflicts in most cases, the secondary-section must provide a storage mapping to avoid conflicts or overriding values from multiple config-sections.

To avoid the sort of potential lockups that you describe I am planning changing the auto-flusher to to try to acquire a non-clocking lock on  the registry before attempting a flush so that if it's busy then it will not wait and grab it the second it becomes free. This means that if the auto-flusher tries to flush at the same moment that someone else is holding a registry lock (probably the monitoring thread but perhaps a parallel submission thread) it will just have to some back again in 30 seconds.

The problem is that the behavior of ujson is arguably broken and lets a developer accidentally serialize things that shouldn't be serializable. Any arbitrary object will be serialized by using its `__dict__`. But what I'm most worried about is that we would serialize a bytestring that seems OK until by chance it contains some non-UTF-8 encoded text and then failing.

Personally I don't think this is too much complexity to avoid those kinds of bugs. (The PR only changes ~75ish lines of code.) The only thing that I might consider is trying to localize the complexity to the `utils` module.

I have captured one of our dead sites (with the help of warcprox + docker to try to make it alive locally).
Then, I have tried to replay it with webrecorder when I have found this problem. With the fix, the first request works but I got stuck just after. After having a deeper look, It seems that https://github.com/webrecorder/pywb/blob/master/pywb/warcserver/inputrequest.py#L265 is looking for some magic values inside the AMF, but in our website's webservices are not in the same format, so (I guess) responses are not related to the right RPC call.


Here is our format. The `target` attribute is like your `operation` one and the parameters are the full body. (python's representation after pyamf's parsing):

```
<Envelope amfVersion=0>
    ('/1', <Request target='.atoms.load'>[{'slug': 'ONF828595'}]</Request>)
</Envelope>
```

I have tried to fix it with more generic function (try to store the python's representation in the `query` variable), but without any success for now. To be honest, I am not sure to fully understand how indexation & url matching is working with pyweb & webRecorder.

If you are interested to see more, you can find an example here: https://webrecorder.io/hhardy/amf/20180302183425$br:chrome:53/http://pib.onf.ca/episode/1731/au-revoir-yale

My understanding is that there are two sorts of column types in dataframes;

* Numeric types (intN, float, etc. -- I think boolean also?)

* String types

Right now our logic is like this:

* Get the number of columns in the dataframe. If it's greater than one, allow the user to select any graph type.  Otherwise just offer pie and table.

I propose changing the logic to be like this:

* Filter out all the string columns in the dataframe and get the number of numeric columns.  If it's greater than one, allow the user to select any graph type. Otherwise just offer pie and table.

I think this should do it.

Thanks for the input. I can see at least two common cases where someone might choose ESP templates over Django templates:

1. The "bulk transactional" case @avelis described above, where you want to issue a single API call to the ESP that generates multiple emails. (Usually for performance reasons.)

2. You're already using your ESP's templating for (say) marketing email, and the people who developed those templates will also develop your transactional templates. And those people know your ESP's template system, not Django's. (Or they want the ability to update the email templates more often than you want to deploy your Django project.)

In either of these cases, I think Anymail can give the ability to identify an ESP template_id and substitution/merge variables for that template. There are still a handful of differences between how ESPs handle variables, which I'll try to list in a later comment, but the general approach and simple template variables would be very portable between ESPs.

The one case this *doesn't* cover is where your Django code supplies the actual body of the template *in the ESP's template language* (rather than just an ID to a stored template already at your ESP). Some ESPs support this, others don't. But I'm leaning toward ignoring it for Anymail. We definitely don't want to get into transcoding template syntax (which I think was being discussed in #10), and if you're writing template content in your Django code, it seems like you'd be better off just using Django templates.

That's intentional – a "yield" (which has now been renamed "checkpoint") is two things: a schedule point and a cancel point. In general they are supposed to happen together, but a test function has to assume that the code it's testing might be buggy, so `assert_yields` is written to assert that both a schedule point and a cancel point happen, and `assert_no_yields` is written to assert that neither happens. (And if just one of them happens, then that's a bug, and they both raise an error on that.)

However, I couldn't come up with any real uses for `assert_no_yields`. And discussing this is sorta pointless without a use case – first because it's impossible to say if it's doing what we want when we don't know what it's supposed to be accomplishing, and second because I deprecated it :-)

Do you have a use case for `assert_no_yields`?

- I didn't assume that dataset names can be duplicate. That is because the idea of `dataset` in alphatwirl originally came from `component` in heppy. Heppy components never have duplicate names because they are used as directory names.
- Now, a dataset in alphatwirl can be generally considered as a set of files. I don't see a reason why not the duplicate is not allowed. It should be allowed.
- What should happen when names are duplicate?
    - Because the dataset names become a column of the output dataframe, in order for the output to make sense, all datasets with the same name should be considred as the same dataset. If datasets with the same name are treated as different datasets, categoreis in the output will have duplicate entreis, which won't makse sense.
